message:
perf(btree): simplify count_all_rows_iterative with explicit pending stack

The iterative count walk had grown a complex per-frame state — a
stack of `(page_no, next_cell_idx, cell_count, right_child,
header_size)` tuples that "remembered where we were" inside each
interior page so the next child could be fetched on demand. That
shape made sense as a way to avoid materializing all child page
numbers up front, but it had two practical costs:

1. Each child traversal re-read the parent page from the pager and
   re-extracted the cell pointer for the next slot. That's a
   pager hit (often a hot-cache hit, but still a virtual-call,
   bounds-check, and cell-pointer-decode pass) per child, even
   though the parent page's content is invariant for the duration
   of the count.
2. The state machine that walked back up the stack — handling the
   "out of cells, switch to right-child, then pop" case — was
   non-trivial: an inner `loop { ... }` with mutable references
   to the top stack frame, manual `cell_idx` increment, and an
   `Option<PageNumber>` to encode "right child not yet visited".
   Easy to misread and slightly slower in profiles than the
   naïve approach.

Refactor to an explicit "pending pages" stack using `SmallVec`:

- One `pending_pages: SmallVec<[PageNumber; 16]>` initialized with
  the root page. Inline 16 elements covers the depth of any btree
  we'd realistically encounter without spilling to the heap.
- The main loop is `while let Some(current_page) = pending_pages.
  pop()`. Each iteration reads the page once, totals the cells
  for leaves (and for interior pages with `cell_count >= 0` —
  unchanged from before), and for interior pages, pushes the
  right child plus every cell child onto the pending stack in
  *reverse* order so that a subsequent `pop()` returns them in
  forward order.
- Reading interior children happens once, in a tight `for cell_idx
  in (0..cell_count).rev()` loop over the parent's cell pointer
  array. The parent's `page_bytes` is borrowed once and reused for
  every cell pointer + child page-number decode. No second pager
  read for the same parent.

The reverse-order push keeps the visit order identical to the
previous implementation (left-to-right cell traversal then
right-child), which matters for any side-effects observable
through `note_page_visit` — same set of pages, same order, same
totals.

Net effect: the count walk does roughly half as many pager reads
on interior-heavy trees (one per page instead of one per
parent-child traversal), and the state-machine collapse makes the
function ~30 lines shorter and noticeably easier to read.

No behavior change. The only difference observable from outside
this function is the new (reduced) page-read count, which is a
pure win.
